item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a fun and entertaining ride.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is up there in the genius pun title category with Disney on Ice, but I'll have to second 11cookeaw1: 23 CM of steel, while certainly impressive, is just too little. Those bullets are incredibly dense and are traveling extremely fast. They would go through steel like so much balsa wood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole time i was just thinking "In Soviet Russia, gun fires you!!" great fun, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Best thing about this is the title (and the whole thing is great). I love the optional read in the invoice. A solid skip with not much to possibly dislike here.

Nitpick
– hide block

There is one inconsistency in the syntactical formatting of the footnotes with regard to punctuation. In all cases but one ("were unsuccessful.4"), the footnote precedes the punctuation. Not enough to consider a typo, so I didn't fix it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More energy then a tank round in a much smaller pacakage.
23cm is too small.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While you aren't necessarily wrong, the article does go out of its way to state that the -A bullets are not harmed via routine firing. The researchers must have been able to recover them intact in order to load one into -2, and it is explicitly stated at the end of the article that the -A used was later found in cleanup. Honestly, I preferred the blacked out number and would like to see the word "plate" changed to something else (block maybe?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 23cm isn't unreasonable depending on the tensile and hardness properties of the -A instances, the type of target being used, and the distance to the target. These are the sort of energies where back-of-the-envelope idealized calculations like the Krupp equation start to fall apart: metals start acting like incompressible fluids, projectiles deform on impact before they can penetrate, that sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shame that people who know general math are being ignored when it comes to how far the bullet travels through the steel block.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know it would do little, but I still want to see it fired at SCP-682.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A normal .50 BMG bullet can penetrate over 20mm of steel at close to mid range.
These bullets are typically going to be 1500 times denser.
That gives us roughly 30-40 meters of steel penetration.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

I got a chance to talk to kaktus about his headcanon for this piece. Apparently, the fine folks at the Helsing and Wulf Rifle Company are suppliers for MC&D, information that could be uncovered by MTF Mu-3. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

The following tests were performed on a sanctioned firing range at Site-71. Attempts to contact the supposed manufacturer of SCP-2818, the Helsing and Wulf Rifle Company, were unsuccessful. With no previous knowledge of SCP-2818's capabilities, the extent of SCP-2818's nature was not known until the initial testing.

To this:

The following tests were performed on a sanctioned firing range at Site-71. Attempts to contact the supposed manufacturer of SCP-2818, the Helsing and Wulf Rifle Company, were unsuccessful.1 With no previous knowledge of SCP-2818's capabilities, the extent of SCP-2818's nature was not known until the initial testing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Operatives of MTF Mu-3 ("Highest Bidders") have confirmed the company to be a supplier for Marshall, Carter and Dark LTD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noting that I changed this:

Round is embedded roughly ███cm deep in a steel plate

To:

Round is embedded roughly 23cm deep in a steel plate.

The black boxes were added not by the author but by another individual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be something like 3.2 cm rather than a three-digit number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Round is embedded roughly ███cm deep in a steel plate

More like a steel block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the invoice supposed to say "discrete shipping", as opposed to "discreet shipping"?

item.title: Error in the article
item.content:encoded:
A 80 kilogram man travelling at 900m/s would 32.4MJ of energy, thats nearly 3 times that of a 120mm tank round, an in a MUCH smaller package, 23cm of steel wouldn't even slow it down. 23 METERS of steel probably wouldn't stop it.
Do remember that said bullet is 500x denser and 2000x heavier then a normal 50cal bullet.

item.title: Silly and amusing
item.content:encoded:
This is funny. An SCP that solves wars by turning the triggerman/woman into the bullet. This made me laugh. I like it.

The fact that the math adds up is impressive. Kudos to the writer.

It seems silly to think that an SCP has the power to turn a person into a projectile. But at the same time its funny to think about because it means that if you really want to end a war, give the enemy these and let them use them a few times.

Upvoted because its making me giggle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if this was made by the same guys who built The Claw. Both are deliciously over the top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so ridiculous, but I love it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me this would be more interesting if the final D-Class also "entered" the bullet, because I like the idea that each bullet just gets more and more powerful each time it's fired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see this as a good idea which could be further improved upon, it feels rather unfinished.
Are there any anomalous effects when you shoot someone with it? Perhaps you could explore that possibility?

I'm hovering between the no-vote and the upvote, will read it again tomorrow and decide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me, I particularly like the 'Anti-Humanity Rifle'.
One should not treat others in ways that one would not like to be treated…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a bit iffy on it until I got to test 20. Not really sure what exactly happened there but it was entertaining. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, yeah, I was reading a comma after "human being".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that's the case I actually might not mind being left a stationary bullet for all time, especially if you get to go out in a literal blaze of glory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, you misread it.

These rounds, classified as SCP-2818-A, maintain a weight and density appropriate of a human being compressed into a space 18.97cm3 in volume.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You say that the bullets have the same weight and density of the original human, but compressed into a bullet. That's… kinda contradictory. Density is mass/volume, so if the volume decreases, the density increases.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the electrical activity, along with the footnote about it, intended to imply the instances are somehow still conscious?

item.title: In Soviet Russia...
item.content:encoded:
Gun fires you!
Sorry, I had to… It is of course a wonderful skip with an interesting and suitably strange effect. So +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this so much. It's utterly brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why you always read the warning labels, kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone during test 20 most definitely said "Oops."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading the collapsible, the only thing that went through my head was:

"Well that just happened."

But seriously though, this was a pretty interesting read.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it when I saw it in chat, and I'm a fan of the changes you've made since. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is kind of silly but I had fun doing the math so hey whaddya gonna do.

Thanks to everybody in sidechats and 19 who checked it out and gave critique (specifically you, Rume). Much appreciated!
